overview.name: Bicycle
overview.description: The Indian bicycle industry is one of the largest globally, producing millions of units annually for both domestic use and international export. It caters to diverse needs, from affordable utility bicycles for daily commuting in rural and urban areas to high-performance mountain, road, and electric bikes for fitness and recreation. India ranks as one of the world's largest bicycle manufacturers, with a production capacity exceeding 15 million units per year.
overview.origin_in_india: Ludhiana in Punjab is often referred to as the 'Bicycle Capital of India', hosting approximately 80% of India's bicycle manufacturing units. Other significant manufacturing centers include Chennai (Tamil Nadu), Sonepat (Haryana), Noida and Ghaziabad (Uttar Pradesh), Kanpur, Mumbai, Kolkata, Patna, and Rajpura (Punjab).
key_organisations: TI Cycles (BSA, Hercules, Montra, Philips, Roadeo, Track & Trail)
overview.historical_note: The bicycle industry in India originated in 1939 with Hind Cycles Ltd. (a Birla company) and Hindustan Bicycle Corporation. Major manufacturing activities accelerated after independence, with companies like Atlas Cycle Industries Ltd. (1950), Hero Cycles Ltd. (1951), and Avon Cycles Ltd. (1952) coming into existence. Atlas Cycles introduced India's first racing bicycle in 1978 and was the official supplier for the 1982 Asian Games in Delhi.

raw_materials.name: Steel
raw_materials.source_region: Odisha, Chhattisgarh, Jharkhand, Karnataka (for raw steel); Ludhiana (for tubes)
raw_materials.cost_per_unit: Rs 80-150 per kg (for bicycle-grade steel tubes, estimated)
raw_materials.notes: Primarily used for frames, forks, handlebars, and other structural components, especially in utility and entry-level bicycles. Atlas Cycles established a steel tube mill in Gurgaon for self-sufficiency.

raw_materials.name: Aluminum
raw_materials.source_region: Odisha, Chhattisgarh (for raw aluminum); Ludhiana, Chennai (for tubes/components)
raw_materials.cost_per_unit: Rs 200-350 per kg (for bicycle-grade aluminum tubes, estimated)
raw_materials.notes: Used for lighter frames, rims, and components, especially in mid-to-high end and performance bicycles.

raw_materials.name: Rubber
raw_materials.cost_per_unit: Rs 230-260 per kg (for natural rubber, as of June 2026)
raw_materials.notes: Essential for tires, inner tubes, handlebar grips, and pedal treads. Both natural and synthetic rubber are used.

raw_materials.name: Plastics
raw_materials.cost_per_unit: Rs 80-150 per kg (for various plastic grades, estimated)
raw_materials.notes: Used for mudguards, chain guards, reflectors, some pedal bodies, cable housings, and saddle bases.

raw_materials.name: Carbon Fiber
raw_materials.source_region: Primarily imported, some processing in India
raw_materials.cost_per_unit: Rs 2000-5000+ per kg (for composite materials, estimated)
raw_materials.notes: Used in high-end performance bicycles for lightweight frames and components. Montra introduced India's first carbon frame bike.

supply_chain.location: Odisha, Chhattisgarh, Jharkhand, Karnataka (steel, aluminum); Kerala, Tamil Nadu (rubber); Gujarat, Maharashtra (plastics)
supply_chain.description: Extraction of iron ore, bauxite, crude oil, and rubber latex. Primary processing involves converting these into steel billets, aluminum ingots, rubber sheets/blocks, and plastic granules. Steel tubes for frames are then manufactured.

supply_chain.location: Ludhiana (Punjab), Noida (Uttar Pradesh), Ghaziabad (Uttar Pradesh), Chennai (Tamil Nadu), Sonepat (Haryana)
supply_chain.description: Specialized units manufacture various bicycle components. This includes frame fabrication (cutting, shaping, welding of steel/aluminum tubes), production of rims, spokes, hubs, gears, chains, pedals, brakes, handlebars, saddles, and tires/tubes. Many MSMEs in Ludhiana contribute to this stage.

supply_chain.location: Ludhiana (Punjab), Chennai (Tamil Nadu), Sonepat (Haryana), Rajpura (Punjab), Sahibabad (Uttar Pradesh), Patna (Bihar)
supply_chain.description: All manufactured and sourced components are brought to assembly plants. Frames undergo painting and finishing. Components like wheels, handlebars, drivetrains, brakes, and saddles are then fitted onto the frame on assembly lines. Final adjustments, quality checks, and packaging are performed.

supply_chain.location: Pan-India, with major distribution hubs in Delhi, Mumbai, Bangalore, Chennai, Kolkata
workers_involved: Cycle mechanics (for assembly and service at retail points)
supply_chain.description: Finished bicycles are transported from manufacturing units to regional distribution centers and then to various retail channels. These include multi-brand bicycle stores, exclusive brand showrooms (e.g., Track & Trail, Hero Lectro stores), large format retail chains (e.g., Decathlon), and online e-commerce platforms. Local cycle mechanics play a crucial role in final assembly and after-sales service.

trade_routes: Road transport networks connect manufacturing hubs (e.g., Ludhiana) to major consumption centers across India.
Rail freight is used for bulk transportation of raw materials and finished goods.

Sea routes from ports like Chennai, Mumbai, and Kolkata are used for international exports and imports of components and high-end bicycles. Ludhiana is landlocked, requiring extensive road/rail connectivity to ports.

economics.retail_price_inr: Rs 4,000 - 2,00,000+ (depending on type, brand, and features)
economics.gst_notes: As of September 2025, the GST rate on non-motorized bicycles (HSN 8712) and all bicycle parts and accessories (HSN 8714) was rationalized to 5%. This was reduced from a previous rate of 12% to promote affordability and sustainable mobility.
economics.market_size_india: USD 3.08 Billion in 2025, projected to reach USD 5.06 Billion by 2034, growing at a CAGR of 5.65% from 2026-2034.

retail_prices.product_name: Hero Sprint Blunt Mountain Bike 29T S/Speed DUALDISC

sustainability.carbon_footprint: Manufacturing processes, especially steel and aluminum production, are energy-intensive and contribute to carbon emissions. Transportation across the supply chain also adds to the footprint. The shift towards e-bicycles also has implications for battery production and disposal.
key_concerns: Resource depletion from raw material extraction (metals, rubber)
Energy consumption in manufacturing, particularly for welding, painting, and component production
Waste generation from manufacturing processes (metal scrap, paint sludge, plastic waste)
End-of-life disposal of bicycles and components, especially non-recyclable parts
Lack of dedicated cycling infrastructure in many Indian cities, hindering wider adoption and safety.
certifications_schemes: ZED (Zero Defect Zero Effect) certifications (for some manufacturers like Eastman Industries).
Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS) for quality standards.

key_schemes: Smart Cities Mission: Supports sustainable urban development and provisions for cycling infrastructure. Includes initiatives like Cycles4Change Challenge and Streets4People Challenge to promote cycling-friendly infrastructure in over 100 cities.
National Urban Transport Policy (NUTP): Emphasizes the implementation of Non-Motorised Transport (NMT) infrastructure.
Public Bicycle Sharing (PBS) schemes: Promoted in various cities to increase bicycle access and adoption.

Government tenders for bicycles: States like Bihar, Karnataka, Chhattisgarh, Tamil Nadu, Assam, West Bengal, and Kerala procure basic, low-cost bicycles for distribution to low-income sections of society.
Make in India initiative: Encourages domestic manufacturing of bicycles and components.
government_policy.regulatory_body: Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS) for quality and safety standards. Industry associations like AICMA and UCPMA advocate for the sector.
government_policy.import_export_policy: India is a significant exporter of bicycle components to global markets (Europe, North America, Southeast Asia, Africa). There has been an increasing trend of imported bicycles and parts, with imports growing faster than exports. The government aims to boost domestic manufacturing and reduce reliance on imports.
